claims.claim_text: Reverse-mode automatic differentiation is the de-facto/default method for neural network training because neural-network losses usually have far fewer outputs than inputs.
claims.rationale: The official PyTorch autograd overview states that reverse mode is the de-facto choice for this output-to-input ratio, but `pytorch.org` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Reverse-mode automatic differentiation and backpropagation are closely related ideas, with autodiff being the broader derivative-computation framework.
claims.rationale: The survey explicitly says automatic differentiation is more general than backpropagation and discusses the relationship between the two communities.

claims.claim_text: A dynamic autograd system such as PyTorch eager execution builds the computation graph as code runs, which enables arbitrary Python control flow and more straightforward debugging than static-graph workflows.
claims.rationale: The official PyTorch README directly describes dynamic neural networks, reverse-mode autodiff, and imperative/debuggable execution.

claims.claim_text: Gradient checkpointing trades compute for memory by not keeping some forward-pass tensors alive and recomputing them during the backward pass.
claims.rationale: PyTorch's checkpoint implementation docstring directly states this compute-versus-memory tradeoff and the backward-pass recomputation behavior.

claims.claim_text: PyTorch accumulates gradients by default, so training loops need `optimizer.zero_grad()` or `model.zero_grad()` each step to avoid carrying old gradients into later updates.
claims.rationale: The official tutorial explicitly says gradients are accumulated by default whenever `.backward()` is called and explains zeroing them in the training loop.

claims.claim_text: Calling `.backward()` twice on the same graph requires retaining the graph; otherwise PyTorch frees the graph/intermediates after backward.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes (row about calling `.backward()` twice without `retain_graph=True`)
claims.rationale: The `torch.autograd.backward` docstring in the PyTorch source states that if `retain_graph` is false, the graph used to compute gradients will be freed.

further_reading.title: Automatic Differentiation in Machine Learning: a Survey
further_reading.why_relevant: Best high-level reference for the relationship between backpropagation, reverse-mode AD, and autodiff implementation styles.

further_reading.title: Training Deep Nets with Sublinear Memory Cost
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical paper behind gradient checkpointing and the compute-memory tradeoff mentioned in the module.

notes: Existing bottom-of-module sources on Linnainmaa and AlexNet do not back the PyTorch-specific inline claims that actually need citation here; those claims are better anchored to official PyTorch source/docs. No war stories, incidents, pricing, or security-event claims in this module required salvage.
